title: Chapter 8 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Classes, Objects, and Instance Variables!

questions.question: What is the relationship between a Class and an Object?
options: A Class is an instance of an Object.
They are the exact same thing.
A Class is a blueprint. An Object is the actual item built from that blueprint.
A Class holds data, an Object holds methods.
questions.explanation: You write a `class` once to define the rules, then use it to generate as many Objects as you need.

questions.question: How should you correctly name a Ruby class?
questions.explanation: Ruby perfectly mandates that Class names must start with a Capital letter and use CamelCase.

questions.question: Which method officially generates a brand new Object using a Class blueprint?
questions.explanation: Calling `.new` (like `Dog.new`) instantiates a fresh Object in memory.

questions.question: What happens when you create two exact objects from the same class? E.g., `a = Car.new` and `b = Car.new`
options: They share the exact same memory.
They are two completely independent, unique Objects with their own private data.
Ruby throws an error for duplicate objects.
questions.explanation: Every time you call `.new`, a fresh walled garden of memory is created. They do not share state.

questions.question: To attach permanent data (State) to an Object (instead of just to a tiny method), you use an Instance Variable. What does an Instance Variable strictly start with?
questions.explanation: The `@` symbol glues the variable permanently to the Object itself (e.g., `@name`).

questions.question: Instance variables (like `@age`) can be accessed and edited directly by ANY code anywhere in the program.
questions.explanation: Instance variables are incredibly secure. The outside world cannot read or edit them without going through special Getter/Setter methods.

questions.question: Which insanely magical method is automatically triggered by Ruby the exact moment you call `.new` on a class?
questions.explanation: If a method is named exactly `initialize`, Ruby automatically runs it to set up the object instantly upon creation.

questions.question: If your `initialize` method asks for `(name)`, what happens if you try to build the object with `Dog.new()` (meaning 0 arguments given)?
options: It crashes with a strict ArgumentError.
It uses a default blank string.
questions.explanation: The `.new` method feeds its arguments directly into `initialize`. If they don't match perfectly, it's a fatal error.

questions.question: What is the primary superpower of an Instance Method?
options: It has full, automatic access to all `@` instance variables within its shared Object.
questions.explanation: Because `@` variables and instance methods both live inside the exact same Object memory, the methods can always read and use the variables seamlessly.

questions.question: How do you trigger an instance method belonging to an object named `my_car`?
questions.explanation: The dot `.` operator is universally used to access an object's internal methods.

questions.question: A method whose sole purpose is to allow the outside world to READ an otherwise-locked internal variable is called a _____ method.
questions.explanation: A Getter explicitly fetches or 'gets' the variable value and hands it to the outside program.

questions.question: If you want the outside world to be able to UPDATE numeric data inside an object, you must build a Setter method. What is the syntax for a Setter method name?
questions.explanation: Ruby uses the sweet syntax `name=` to define a Setter method, allowing the programmer to write `user.age = 22`.

questions.question: What does the `attr_accessor` macro actually do under the hood?
options: It automatically writes the invisible Getter and Setter instance methods for you.
questions.explanation: Using `attr_accessor :username` tells Ruby to invisibly write the `def username` (Getter) and `def username=` (Setter) methods instantly.

questions.question: If you want the outside world to be able to READ data, but you violently NEVER want them to be able to EDIT it, what should you use?
questions.explanation: `attr_reader` only generates the Getter method. It deliberately does not generate the Setter method, making the variable read-only.

questions.question: In Ruby, it is impossible to instantiate an object without manually writing an `initialize` method.
options: True - Without it, the object cannot exist.
False - Ruby provides a completely invisible, empty initialize method perfectly by default.
questions.explanation: If you don't write one, Ruby quietly uses an empty one in the background so `.new` still works.

questions.question: Look at this code: `class Robot; end`. What data type is `Robot`?
questions.explanation: Classes define an entire custom data type! Once created, `Robot` joins the ranks of String and Integer.

questions.question: What is the exact term in Object Oriented Programming for an object's internally stored data characteristics?
questions.explanation: Variables make up an object's 'State'. Methods make up an object's 'Behavior'.

questions.question: Instance methods defined directly inside `class Car` can be used by an `Airplane` object.
options: True - All standard methods are global.
False - Instance methods are physically trapped strictly inside their parent blueprint.
questions.explanation: The methods belong to the Car class! Airplanes know absolutely nothing about them.

questions.question: Fill the blank: `def ______` is the precise spelling of the constructor method in Ruby.
questions.explanation: It must be spelled `initialize` via strict convention. `initialise` with an 's' will not work!

questions.question: If you type `fido = Dog.new`, what is `fido`?
options: An instance (object) of the Dog class saved into a parameter variable.
questions.explanation: `fido` is a local variable holding the physical Object in memory that was generated by `.new`.
